steps.title: Find the y-intercept of f(x)
steps.content:
Set $x = 0$ in $f(x) = a(2.2^x + 2.2^b)$:
$$f(0) = a(2.2^0 + 2.2^b) = a(1 + 2.2^b)$$
This is the y-intercept of $f(x)$ that we need to match in the alternative forms.

steps.title: Check Form I: can we display $a(1 + 2.2^b)$ as a constant or coefficient?
steps.content:
If $g(x) = a(2.2^x + k)$ is equivalent to $f(x)$, then $k = 2.2^b$.

Expanding: $g(x) = a \cdot 2.2^x + ak = a \cdot 2.2^x + a \cdot 2.2^b$

The explicit constants/coefficients in $g(x) = a(2.2^x + k)$ are $a$ and $k$.

The y-intercept is $a(1 + 2.2^b) = a + a \cdot 2.2^b$, which equals neither $a$ alone nor $k = 2.2^b$ alone. It is a product of coefficients ($a(1 + 2.2^b)$), not a single coefficient.

Form I does not display the y-intercept as a constant or coefficient.

steps.title: Check Form II: can we display $a(1 + 2.2^b)$ as a constant or coefficient?
steps.content:
If $h(x) = a(2.2)^x + m$ is equivalent to $f(x) = a(2.2^x + 2.2^b)$, then:
$$a(2.2)^x + m = a(2.2)^x + a \cdot 2.2^b$$
So $m = a \cdot 2.2^b$.

The explicit constants/coefficients are $a$ and $m = a \cdot 2.2^b$.

The y-intercept is $h(0) = a + m = a + a \cdot 2.2^b = a(1 + 2.2^b)$.

This y-intercept is a sum of two coefficients ($a + m$), not a single coefficient. The value $m$ alone equals $a \cdot 2.2^b$, which is not equal to the y-intercept $a(1 + 2.2^b)$.

Form II does not display the y-intercept as a constant or coefficient.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Neither form displays the y-intercept of $f(x)$ as a single constant or coefficient. In Form I, the y-intercept is a product of coefficients. In Form II, it is a sum of coefficients. That confirms D is correct.
